coredata.dc:title: Visual and conventional Off-On mode platform for detection of Ofloxacin in water and Eye-Drop using fluorescence enhancement and redshift of Sumac-Derived carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fatah, Shilan A.
Omer, Khalid M.
coredata.dc:description: Ofloxacin (OFL), a broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is widely utilized for treating bacterial infections. Accurate detection of OFL is essential for pharmaceutical quality control and environmental monitoring, particularly in wastewater. This study introduces a novel fluorescence-based detection method using sumac-derived carbon dots (S-CDs), synthesized via a simple hydrothermal approach. The S-CDs exhibited spherical morphology (20–30 nm) with blue fluorescence emission and a quantum yield of 1.4 %. Upon interaction with OFL, a remarkable fluorescence enhancement was observed, resulting in a 45-fold increase in quantum yield (45.0 %) accompanied by a red shift in the emission. This strong enhancement and red-shift emission enabled to design off–on mode for detection of OFL. The fluorescence intensity showed a strong linear correlation with OFL concentration (R2 = 0.98) in the range of 1–90 nM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.7 nM and a limit of quantification of 2.3 nM. Furthermore, a smartphone-assisted visual detection method was developed based on an off–on fluorescence mechanism. RGB analysis enabled real-time quantification of OFL, exhibiting a linear detection range of 20–80 µM with an LOD 0.97 µM. The method was successfully applied to detect OFL in eye drop formulations and tap water, demonstrating high accuracy and precision. These findings underscore the potential of S-CDs as a cost-effective, portable, and efficient tool for OFL detection, suitable for pharmaceutical and environmental monitoring applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ofloxacin
Fluorescence enhancement
Biosensing
Smartphone analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH sensitivity and ionic strength stability
Smartphone-Based detection of OFL using fluorescent S-CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural and chemical characterization of S-CDs. (A) TEM image showing well-dispersed, spherical S-CDs with particle sizes below 150 nm. (B) Particle size distribution histogram revealing a narrow size range, with most particles averaging between 20–30 nm, confirming uniform nanoscale morphology. (C) XRD patterns of S-CDs (blue) and OFL–S-CDs (red), both exhibiting broad diffraction peaks characteristic of amorphous carbon structures; the slight variation between the two indicates possible surface interactions between S-CDs and OFL. (D) FTIR spectrum of S-CDs, showing prominent absorption bands at 3447.95 cm−1 (O–H/N–H stretching), 2928.42 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 2080.42 cm−1 (C≡N), 1638.42 cm−1 (C=O stretching), 1166.92 cm−1 (S=O/C–O), and 645.72 cm−1 (C–S), confirming the presence of diverse functional groups on the surface that contribute to solubility, stability, and fluorescence behavior. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Optical characterization and interaction analysis of S-CDs with OFL. (A) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of S-CDs, displaying a clear Stokes shift with maximum absorption at ∼ 365 nm and emission at ∼ 445 nm. (B) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of S-CDs at wavelengths from 330 to 400 nm, highlighting excitation-dependent behavior. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of S-CDs, OFL, and their complex (OFL–S-CDs), showing significant fluorescence enhancement upon OFL addition; inset: visual fluorescence under UV light. (D) Fluorescence spectra of OFL–S-CDs at increasing OFL concentrations (1–90 nM), indicating concentration-dependent enhancement. (E) Calibration curve for OFL detection using OFL–S-CDs. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three independent measurements (n = 3), confirming the reproducibility and reliability of the sensor response. (F) UV–Vis spectra of S-CDs, OFL, and OFL–S-CDs, revealing spectral overlap and confirming interaction. (G–I) Zeta potential measurements: (G) S-CDs (−31 mV), (H) OFL–S-CDs (−15 mV), and (I) overlaid profiles showing a shift in surface charge, supporting successful electrostatic interaction and complex formation.

Stability evaluation of S-CDs and their complex with OFL–S-CDs under varying pH and ionic strength conditions. (A) Fluorescence intensity of S-CDs (blue squares) and OFL–S-CDs complexes (red squares) measured across a pH range of 2 to 12. Error bars represent standard deviation from three independent measurements (n = 3), demonstrating high reproducibility. (B) Photographs of OFL–S-CDs solutions under 365 nm UV light at various pH levels (2–10). (C) Fluorescence intensity of S-CDs recorded in the presence of increasing NaCl concentrations (0–3.0 M). Error bars represent standard deviation from triplicate measurements (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Smartphone-Assisted Visual Quantification of OFL Using S-CDs Under UV Illumination. (A) Schematic representation of the detection procedure: OFL–S-CDs solutions were exposed to UV light (365 nm) and photographed using a smartphone. The resulting images were analyzed via RGB extraction software to quantify fluorescence intensity. (B) Visual fluorescence response of OFL–S-CDs at various OFL concentrations (0–200 µM) under UV light, showing a gradual increase in green fluorescence with higher concentrations. (C) RGB analysis of the captured images, revealing a concentration-dependent increase in green channel values, accompanied by a decrease in red and blue components, enabling visual differentiation of OFL levels. (D) Calibration curve plotting green fluorescence intensity against OFL concentration (20–80 µM), exhibiting a strong linear relationship (Y = 14.2089x + 6.4176, R2 = 0.9808). Error bars denote standard deviations from three independent measurements (n = 3), confirming the method’s sensitivity, reproducibility, and suitability for smartphone-based OFL detection. (E) Calibration curve for low OFL concentrations (1–10 µM), used to evaluate detection sensitivity. The linear regression equation (Y = 91.977x + 2.609; R2 = 0.98) indicates excellent linearity. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Selectivity and interference analysis of the S-CDs–OFL fluorescence sensing system. (A) Antibiotic selectivity: Green fluorescence intensity (green value) of S-CDs in the presence of structurally similar antibiotics (tetracycline, levofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, and ofloxacin). Error bars represent the standard deviation from three independent measurements (n = 3). (B) Metal ion interference: Fluorescence intensities of the S-CDs–OFL complex in the presence of various metal ions and anions (Fe2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−). Error bars denote standard deviations from triplicate experiments (n = 3). (C) Biomolecular interference: Fluorescence response of S-CDs–OFL in the presence of biologically relevant molecules (L-Argine, glycine, fructose, lactose, uria, uric acid, glucose, ascorbic acid, and aspartic acid). Error bars reflect standard deviation from three measurements (n = 3). (D) UV visualization (365 nm): Photographic images showing fluorescence of S-CDs with different metal ions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Presentation shows preparation of S-CDs from sumac and using in OFF-ON detection design for OFL in different samples.
. Comparing CDs used for detection of OFL using other methods.
Spike Recovery for detection of OFL in real samples based on the proposed off–on sensing platform.

head.ce:title: Visual and conventional Off-On mode platform for detection of Ofloxacin in water and Eye-Drop using fluorescence enhancement and redshift of Sumac-Derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science University of Sulaimani, Qliasan Street, Sulaymaniyah 46002 Kurdistan region, Iraq

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science University of Sulaimani

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science University of Sulaimani, Qliasan Street, Sulaymaniyah, 46002, Kurdistan region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ofloxacin (OFL), a broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is widely utilized for treating bacterial infections. Accurate detection of OFL is essential for pharmaceutical quality control and environmental monitoring, particularly in wastewater. This study introduces a novel fluorescence-based detection method using sumac-derived carbon dots (S-CDs), synthesized via a simple hydrothermal approach. The S-CDs exhibited spherical morphology (20–30 nm) with blue fluorescence emission and a quantum yield of 1.4 %. Upon interaction with OFL, a remarkable fluorescence enhancement was observed, resulting in a 45-fold increase in quantum yield (45.0 %) accompanied by a red shift in the emission. This strong enhancement and red-shift emission enabled to design off–on mode for detection of OFL. The fluorescence intensity showed a strong linear correlation with OFL concentration (R2 = 0.98) in the range of 1–90 nM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.7 nM and a limit of quantification of 2.3 nM. Furthermore, a smartphone-assisted visual detection method was developed based on an off–on fluorescence mechanism. RGB analysis enabled real-time quantification of OFL, exhibiting a linear detection range of 20–80 µM with an LOD 0.97 µM. The method was successfully applied to detect OFL in eye drop formulations and tap water, demonstrating high accuracy and precision. These findings underscore the potential of S-CDs as a cost-effective, portable, and efficient tool for OFL detection, suitable for pharmaceutical and environmental monitoring applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Detection of antibiotics in pharmaceutical, biological and environmental samples are critical for ensuring food safety, public health, and effective disease management [1]. Ofloxacin (OFL), a widely used fluoroquinolone antibiotic, plays a significant role in treating bacterial infections. However, its misuse and over-prescription have led to increased concerns about antibiotic residues and resistance [2]. Therefore, developing reliable biosensing methods for detecting ofloxacin is essential for effective therapeutic monitoring and environmental protection.

Many methods are available for detection of OFL in various samples, such as UV–Vis spectrophotometry [3], high performance liquid chromatography [4], mass spectrometry [5], and electrochemical methods [6,7]. are commonly used for OFL quantification. HPLC, in particular, remains a gold standard due to its high specificity, accuracy, and ability to separate complex mixtures using advanced analytical columns. However, while these methods are powerful in laboratory settings, they often require expensive equipment, skilled personnel, and labor-intensive sample preparation. Additionally, their limited portability makes them less suited for rapid or on-site analysis, especially in resource-limited or field environments.To complement these standard techniques, there is increasing interest in alternative sensing platforms that are simpler, low-cost, and environmentally friendly.

From material’s perspective, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a versatile platform for biosensing applications due to their unique optical properties, biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization [8,9]. These nanomaterials, characterized by their small size and high surface area, can serve as efficient signal transducers in biosensors, enabling the sensitive and selective detection of various biomolecules and drugs [10]. The synthesis of carbon dots from natural sources aligns with the principles of green chemistry, making it an attractive approach for sustainable nanomaterial production.

Sumac is the common name for plants in the Rhus genus, which is part of the Anacardiaceae family [11]. Sumac is a small tree or shrub with medicinal properties in its bark, leaves, and berries, known for being a powerful antioxidant, diuretic, and treatment for various ailments like infections, diabetes, and cancer [12]. The root bark is used to treat conditions such as gonorrhea and scrofula, while sumac tea helps with colds, flu, and respiratory issues. Therefore, it is highly important to prepare carbon dots from a wild plant that are available in many places.

From detection perspective, fluorescence-based analysis is a simple, sensitive, and cost-effective method for detecting low concentrations of analytes, making it ideal for rapid and on-site monitoring [13–15]. Its high sensitivity allows for the detection of even trace levels of analytes in complex samples, while its simplicity ensures ease of use, especially when integrated with portable, smartphone-assisted detection devices [16–18]. This makes fluorescence-based analysis a promising tool for practical and real-time applications.

Smartphone-assisted visual-based detection is a significant approach due to its simplicity, affordability, and suitability for on-site applications [14,19,20]. It enables rapid, real-time analysis without the need for complex instrumentation or specialized training, making it ideal for resource-limited settings [21–23]. Key properties include portability, sensitivity, reliability, and environmental friendliness, often enhanced through the integration of fluorescence or colorimetric probes and smartphone-assisted systems. This method provides a cost-effective, user-friendly, and eco-friendly solution for diverse applications, including environmental monitoring, healthcare, and food safety [24,25]. To this end, it is essential to integrate low-cost, natural-based precursor CDs with fluorescence-based visual detection modes and an off–on sensing mechanism into a single probe design.

In this study, we synthesized carbon dots from S-CDs, a widely available natural precursor, and investigated their application for the detection of OFL via a fluorescence enhancement-based OFF–ON sensing mechanism. Upon interaction with OFL, the fluorescence intensity of the S-CDs increased by approximately 45-fold. This significant enhancement enabled the development of a dual-mode detection platform combining conventional fluorometric analysis (instrument-based) with smartphone-assisted visual detection (instrument-free). The proposed system was successfully applied to detect OFL in real-world samples, highlighting its potential as a portable, low-cost, and environmentally friendly tool for antibiotic monitoring. Scheme 1 shows the presentation of preparation and application of S-CDs from natural source with OFL detection.

Sumac (Rhus) was obtained from local markets in Kurdistan, Iraq, and naturally dried before use. Deionized water was utilized for all chemical procedures in the laboratory. The antibiotics used in this study, including Ofloxacin, Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin, and Tetracycline, were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China).

The samples were characterized using a variety of advanced analytical techniques. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a JEM-2100 microscope (from a Japanese company) was employed to examine the microstructure and size distribution. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to identify chemical functional groups, with the instrument provided by Bruker Corporation (Germany). The crystalline structure of the samples was analyzed through X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Bruker AXS D8 Discover diffractometer, also from Bruker (Germany). To investigate the optical properties, a UV–Visible Spectrophotometer T80+ (manufactured by Beckman Instruments, USA) was utilized to measure absorbance and transmittance across various wavelengths, offering insights into the samples’ interaction with UV and visible light. Additionally, the fluorescence properties were analyzed using the Agilent Technologies Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (from Agilent Technologies, USA), which enabled high-sensitivity detection and precise fluorescence intensity measurements at different excitation and emission wavelengths. Together, these instrumental techniques provided a comprehensive evaluation of the samples' optical, structural, and chemical characteristics.

Sumac was chosen as the carbon source for synthesizing S-CDs due to its availability, affordability, safety, and suitability. A sumac sample (2.0200 g) was dispersed in 100 mL of distilled water and heated in a beaker on a hot plate at 130 °C for 10 min, followed by an increase to 140 °C for 20 min. The sumac, sourced from a local spice shop, was thoroughly washed with tap water and then deionized water to eliminate impurities. The hydrothermal synthesis was conducted in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 170 °C for 18 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting brown-yellow solution was filtered and purified by dialysis using a 2000 Da molecular weight cut-off membrane to remove residual impurities. The purified solution was stored at 4 °C for further characterization.

The fluorescence analysis involved a systematic evaluation of fluorescence intensity using an appropriate instrument for precise measurements. In the first step, solutions were prepared for comparison: 0.5 mL of S-CDs solution, diluted S-CDs (0.2 mL of S-CDs mixed with 9.5 mL of distilled water), an OFL solution (20 µM OFL diluted with distilled water), and an OFL-S-CDs complex (0.2 mL of S-CDs combined with 20 µM OFL), all prepared in the presence of 0.1 M citrate buffer to ensure pH stability and enhance fluorescence performance. The fluorescence intensity of each solution was recorded, and comparisons were made to assess their relative fluorescence behavior. In the second step, a series of solutions with varying OFL concentrations (1–90 nM) were prepared while maintaining a fixed S-CDs concentration of 0.2 mL in each solution and 0.1 M citrate buffer, with the total volume adjusted to completion using distilled water. The fluorescence intensity of each solution was recorded, and the data were analyzed to evaluate the impact of varying OFL concentrations on the fluorescence response. This comprehensive analysis provided a detailed understanding of the interaction dynamics between S-CDs and OFL.

A visual-mode detection platform using a smartphone enables rapid, user-friendly, and on-site analysis by capturing and interpreting colorimetric or fluorescence changes through the phone’s camera, eliminating the need for sophisticated instruments [26]. The experimental analysis began with a two-step visual approach under UV light, using a smartphone to capture fluorescence data through RGB (Red, Green, Blue) values. Initially, a set of solutions was prepared for comparison: 0.5 mL of S-CDs solution, diluted S-CDs (0.5 mL of S-CDs mixed with 4.5 mL of distilled water), an OFL solution (0.1 mL of OFL mixed with 4.9 mL of distilled water), and an OFL-S-CDs complex (0.5 mL of S-CDs combined with 0.1 mL of OFL and 4.4 mL of distilled water), all solutions were prepared in the presence of 0.1 M citrate buffer to maintain a stable pH and improve fluorescence visualization. Each solution was exposed to UV light, and digital photographs of the fluorescent solutions were captured. Subsequently, a serial analysis was conducted by preparing solutions with a fixed S-CDs concentration (0.5 mL) and varying OFL concentrations (20–80 µM), adjusted to a final volume of 5 mL. The pH of the S-CDs solution was set at 3.5 using distilled water and 0.1 M citrate buffer. These solutions were similarly analyzed under UV light, and photographs were taken for further evaluation. The RGB values of the images were analyzed using ImageJ software to assess the fluorescence response.

In this study, real samples were collected from two sources: liquid eye drops obtained from a local pharmacy in Kurdistan, Iraq, and tap water from a household supply. To prepare the eye drop samples, 0.1 mL of the solution was incubated at room temperature for 30 min to allow optimal interaction between OFL and S-CDs. After incubation, the mixture was filtered and stored under refrigeration to enhance the stability and effectiveness of OFL for eye infection treatment. Fluorescence intensity was evaluated using UV light and a smartphone-based RGB visual analysis system at varying OFL concentrations while maintaining a fixed S-CD concentration of 200 μM. The total volume was adjusted to 5 mL using distilled water. For fluorescence spectroscopy detection, the eye drop samples were spiked with OFL at concentrations of 5.7, 6.0, and 7.8 μM. Similarly, tap water samples were prepared by thoroughly mixing 3 mL of water with different OFL concentrations while maintaining a fixed S-CD concentration. The prepared solutions contained OFL at concentrations of 6.7, 7.2, and 8.1 μM. These samples were subsequently analyzed using fluorescence spectroscopy and a smartphone-integrated detection platform to assess fluorescence intensity and detect S-CDs-OFL presence.

In this study, distilled water (refractive index = 1.33) was used as the solvent for the carbon dots. The emission spectra of the carbon dots were recorded at an emission wavelength of 350 nm, with excitation at 340 nm for the S-CDs. Quantum yield (QY) was calculated following the F Std 14690.55 procedure, which involves using absorbance values at the excitation wavelength to determine QY. The absorbance of the standard at the excitation wavelength was 0.011, while the absorbance of the S-CDs at the same wavelength was 0.657. The fluorescence standard used for the quantum yield calculation was a fluorophore (fluorescein) with a known quantum yield of 0.92. The QY was determined using the following formula:
QY
sam
= Q Y ×
A
ref
A
sam
×
I
sam
I
ref
×
n
sam
2
n
ref
2

where
QY
sam
and
QY
are the QY of the sample and reference, respectively. Similarly,
A
sam
and
A
ref
are the absorbance of the sample and reference at the optimal excitation wavelength;
I
sam
and
I
ref
are the integral fluorescence intensity of the sample and reference, respectively;
n
sam
and
n
ref
are given by correspond to the refractive indices of the sample dispersion and reference solution [27]. To ensure reliable results, a series of sample and reference solutions with varying concentrations were prepared, maintaining optical absorption values within the range of 0–0.1. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded, and QY were determined by comparing fluorescence intensity with absorbance curves.

In the study of S-CDs, TEM analysis revealed small, spherical particles with sizes ranging from 30 to 150 nm, and an average diameter of approximately 20–30 nm ( Fig. 1
A). The particle size distribution, as illustrated in a histogram ( Fig. 1 B), demonstrated a narrow range, suggesting uniformity in size and shape of the particles. The PXRD ( Fig. 1 C) patterns showed broad peaks, confirming an amorphous structure with low crystallinity [28]. FTIR identified several surface functional groups, with prominent peaks at 3447.95 cm−1 carboxylic groups (–COOH), 2928.42 cm−1 (for C-H stretching), 2080.42 cm−1, 1638.42 cm−1, 1166.52 cm−1, and 645.72 cm−1, which correspond to different functional groups and confirm the surface chemistry of the carbon dots [29]. Which play a crucial role in the chemical reactivity of the carbon dots ( Fig. 1 D). These combined characterization techniques provide a comprehensive understanding of the size, structure, and surface chemistry of the carbon dots.

In this study, the optical properties of S-CDs were investigated in combination with OFL. The absorption and emission spectra of S-CDs display two distinct peaks ( Fig. 2
A): the absorption peak (blue line), corresponding to the π to π* and n to π* transition in the S-CDs. The fluorescence emission peak (red color). The fluorescence intensity of the S-CDs was measured under different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 2B. The minimal variations in the maximum emission wavelengths suggest that the carbon particles have similar sizes and possess identical functional groups on their surfaces. Interestingly, adding OFL to the S-CDs enhances and tunes the emission of both S-CDs (λem 425 nm) and OFL (λem 430 nm) to almost green-yellow region with λem 525 nm (Fig. 2C). The results reveal a notable tune in the emission wavelength and increase in fluorescence intensity in the OFL:S-CDs, indicating that the incorporation of OFL induces new fluorescence properties of the OFL:S-CDs. A substantial difference in light emission efficiency was observed between the S-CDs and OFL-S-CDs. The QY of the S-CDs was determined to be 1.4 %. In contrast, the OFL:S-CDs showed a much higher QY of 45.2 %. This results in the quantum yield of OFL-S-CDs being approximately 45 times higher than that of the S-CDs, demonstrating a significant enhancement in light emission efficiency. This substantial increase in quantum yield underscores the superior fluorescence properties of the OFL-S-CDs, making them a promising material for various applications that require efficient light emission. A concentration-dependent fluorescence response of the OFL–S-CDs system was observed over the range of 1–90 nM, as shown in Fig. 2D. A strong linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and OFL concentration was established within the range of 1–90 nM (Fig. 2E). The calibration curve follows the regression equation Y = 16.9489x + 5.0088Y, with a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.98, indicating excellent linearity. The LOD was determined to be 0.7 nM, demonstrating the system’s high sensitivity. Error bars in the calibration curve represent standard deviations from three independent measurements (n = 3), confirming the reproducibility and reliability of the sensing platform. These results highlight the potential of OFL–S-CDs as a robust and sensitive probe for quantitative detection of OFL at trace levels.

S-CDs to form OFL-S-CDs has significantly enhanced their fluorescence efficiency, as reflected in their quantum yield (QY). The QY of S-CDs is relatively low at 1.02, indicating minimal fluorescence emission relative to absorbed photons. However, after functionalization with OFL, the QY dramatically increases to 45.2, representing a 44.18-fold improvement in fluorescence efficiency. This enhancement is likely due to surface passivation, which reduces non-radiative decay by stabilizing excited-state energy, as well as improved electronic structure and reduced aggregation, which minimize fluorescence quenching. The increased QY makes OFL-S-CDs far more effective for applications requiring high fluorescence intensity, such as bioimaging, biosensing, and fluorescent labeling, where brightness and sensitivity are crucial. This substantial enhancement demonstrates the impact of functionalization in optimizing the optical properties.

of carbon dots for advanced fluorescence-based applications. The UV–Vis absorption spectra display distinct characteristics for each component, with the red curve corresponding to OFL-S-CDs, the blue curve to OFL, and the black curve to S-CDs. These spectra emphasize the unique absorption profiles of each component. However, the graphs indicate no evidence of chemical reactions between OFL and S-CDs (Fig. 2 F).

As reported previously, the electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bond interactions accompanied by charge transfer between S-CDs and OFL molecules typically result in an electron transfer process, which subsequently affects the fluorescence intensity of the OFL:S-CDs [30,31]. To further support this interaction assumption, we performed zeta potential analysis for S-CDs and S-CDs:OFL. In the zeta potential of S-CDs was found to be −31 mV, indicating strong electrostatic stability (Fig. 2 G). However, after interacting with ofloxacin, the zeta potential of OFLO-S-CDs shifted to −15 mV, reflecting a decrease in negative charge ( Fig. 2 H). Fig. 2 I shows both samples on the same graph, with S-CDs in red and OFLO-S-CDs in blue, clearly illustrating the shift in the zeta potential from −31 mV to −15 mV. This indicates that there is a surface interaction between S-CDs and OFL that lead to decrease the zeta potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH sensitivity and ionic strength stability
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A illustrates the influence of pH (ranging from 2 to 12) on the fluorescence intensity S-CDs and the S-CDs-OFL complex. The fluorescence intensity of S-CDs alone (black curve) remains relatively low across the entire pH range, with a slight decrease observed as pH increases. This indicates that S-CDs exhibit weak fluorescence and maintain stability under varying pH conditions. Notably, their fluorescence reaches its peak at pH 3.5, suggesting that a slightly acidic environment enhances their inherent fluorescence properties.

In contrast, the fluorescence of the S-CDs-OFL complex (red curve) is significantly enhanced at lower pH values, with a peak observed between pH 2 and 3. As pH increases, the fluorescence intensity steadily declines, reaching minimal levels in alkaline conditions (pH > 10). This behavior indicates that the interaction between S-CDs and OFL is pH-sensitive, with maximum fluorescence enhancement occurring in strongly acidic environments. The decrease in fluorescence at higher pH values may result from the deprotonation of functional groups on OFL or S-CDs, which disrupts electronic interactions and leads to fluorescence quenching.

These results underscore the critical role of pH in modulating the optical properties of S-CDs and their interactions with OFL. The findings suggest that the optimal pH for detecting OFL using S-CDs is within the acidic range, particularly around pH 2–3, while S-CDs alone exhibit their highest fluorescence at pH 3.5. This insight is valuable for the development of fluorescence-based sensing applications and potential biomedical diagnostics. Fig. 3B demonstrates visually the impact of different pH levels (ranging from 2 to 10) on the fluorescence properties of S-CDs in the presence of Ofloxacin (OFL), with samples excited under UV light to observe fluorescence variations.

These observations align with the fluorescence intensity trends observed in the spectroscopic data, confirming that the S-CDs-OFL complex exhibits the strongest fluorescence in highly acidic conditions (pH 2–4) and weak fluorescence under alkaline conditions. This pH-dependent fluorescence behavior indicates that the S-CDs-OFL complex could serve as a potential fluorescence probe for applications where pH-sensitive detection is required, such as environmental monitoring and biomedical sensing.

Fig. 3C presents the effect of varying NaCl concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs. The fluorescence intensity is plotted against NaCl concentrations ranging from 0.5 M to 3 M, with a blank sample for reference. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs remains largely unaffected by NaCl concentration, showing no significant quenching or enhancement. The fluorescence intensity remains close to 140 across all tested concentrations, suggesting that S-CDs exhibit strong salt stability and are not significantly influenced by ionic strength variations. This stability indicates that S-CDs can maintain their fluorescence properties even in saline environments, making them suitable for use in complex biological or environmental samples where salt interference might occur. These findings highlight the robustness of S-CDs in the presence of NaCl, further supporting their potential use in biosensing and environmental monitoring applications where ionic strength variations are common.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-Based detection of OFL using fluorescent S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a smartphone-based visual analysis was utilized to examine S-CDs-based OFL detection (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4 A, the experiment involved exposing S-CD:OFL solutions to UV light using a handheld UV torch inside a custom-built dark box, leading to fluorescence emission. Then, the fluorescence was analyzed using RGB color analysis to detect variations in green, blue, and red intensities. Fig. 4 B shows the fluorescence response of different OFL concentrations, ranging from 20 to 80 µM. As the OFL concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity also increased, particularly in the green channel. Fig. 4 C demonstrates the RGB analysis of varying concentrations, highlighting a noticeable increase in green values, while blue and red values gradually decreased as the OFL concentration increased. A calibration curve was established by plotting green intensity values against OFL concentrations ( Fig. 4 D), revealing a strong linear correlation (R2 = 0.98). Error bars in the calibration graph represent the standard deviation from three independent measurements (n = 3), confirming the accuracy, precision, and reproducibility of the smartphone-assisted fluorescence quantification approach. It is important to note that successful implementation of smartphone-based fluorescence analysis requires stringent control of external variables that may influence image acquisition. Key factors include maintaining a consistent distance and angle between the smartphone camera and the sample, ensuring uniform lighting conditions inside the dark box, and stabilizing the camera position to avoid variations in intensity measurements. Standardizing these parameters is essential to minimize measurement error and enhance reproducibility in portable fluorescence-based sensing platforms.

To evaluate the method’s sensitivity at lower concentrations, a second calibration curve was constructed for the range of 1–10 µM (Fig. 4 E). This analysis yielded a a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.98). The LOQ was calculated to be 3.2 µM, as identified by the intersection of the regression line with the detection threshold. These results validate the method’s high sensitivity for detecting low levels of OFL.

To ensure accuracy and reproducibility in smartphone-based fluorescence analysis, several parameters must be tightly controlled. These include maintaining a fixed distance and angle between the smartphone and sample, consistent lighting within the dark box, and stabilizing the smartphone to prevent image distortion. Proper standardization of these conditions is critical for minimizing variability and enabling reliable, portable fluorescence detection.

The selectivity of the S-CDs–OFL sensing platform was systematically evaluated against structurally related antibiotics, common metal ions, and biologically relevant molecules to assess its performance in complex environments. As illustrated in Fig. 5
A, the green fluorescence intensity of S-CDs increases markedly upon the addition of OFL, while structurally similar antibiotics—such as tetracycline, levofloxacin, and ciprofloxacin—induce negligible changes. This significant fluorescence enhancement highlights the strong binding affinity and high specificity of S-CDs toward OFL. All values are presented as mean ± standard deviation from triplicate measurements (n = 3), indicating excellent reproducibility.

To assess the effect of potential ionic interference, S-CDs and the S-CDs–OFL complex were exposed to a variety of common metal cations and anions, as shown in Fig. 5 B. In the absence of OFL, several metal ions—most notably Fe2+—quench the fluorescence of S-CDs. However, upon the addition of OFL, the fluorescence intensity is restored across all tested ions. This fluorescence recovery suggests that OFL binding protects the S-CDs from quenching effects and stabilizes their emission, demonstrating the sensor’s robustness in the presence of competing ions.

The platform’s tolerance to interference from small biomolecules was also examined ( Fig. 5 C). A range of amino acids (L-arginine, glycine, aspartic acid), sugars (fructose, lactose), and metabolites (uria, uric acid, glucose, ascorbic acid) were tested. In the absence of OFL, these species caused minimal changes in fluorescence. In contrast, a pronounced and consistent enhancement in fluorescence was observed when OFL was present, confirming the high selectivity of the S-CDs–OFL system against biologically relevant substances.

Visual evidence under UV light (365 nm), shown in Fig. 5 D, further supports these findings. Most samples exhibited bright green fluorescence, with the exception of the Fe2+ sample, which displayed strong fluorescence quenching and a visible brown coloration. These visual observations align with the spectroscopic data, reinforcing the anti-interference capability and practical reliability of the S-CDs–OFL sensing system for selective OFL detection in complex matrices.

For sake of comparison with previous reported papers, Table 1 presents various carbon dot-based materials for fluorescence and visual detection, highlighting their linear detection ranges and LODs. Our as-prepared S-CDs (instrument-based fluorescence) exhibit the lowest LOD (0.7 nM), indicating high sensitivity, while 1,8-CQDs (visual detection) cover the broadest range (0–120 μM). Overall, our S-CDs stand out for their dual detection modes, combining high sensitivity with practical usability.

Due to the strong fluorescence enhancement, off–on sensing platform was used to detect OFL in real samples. We have taken liquid eyes drop formulations and tap water, demonstrating their potential for pharmaceutical and environmental analysis (Table 2 ). Regarding liquid eye drops, the OFL-S-CD composite was evaluated by spiking a liquid eye drop sample with a standard OFL solution. The recovery rate was calculated at 98 %, indicating that the composite effectively enhances the emission. This high recovery rate suggests that S-CDs improve the stability and efficacy of OFL in eye drop formulations, making them a promising tool for ocular treatments.

Regarding tap water: The OFL-S-CD composite was also tested in tap water to assess its performance under real-world environmental conditions. Ion interference from minerals and salts in tap water lowered the fluorescence intensity, resulting in a decrease in the emission signal. Nevertheless, the composite still successfully detected OFL. Spike recovery experiments in tap water produced promising results. When 4.0 µM OFL was spiked into tap water, the measured concentration was 4.1 µM, resulting in a recovery rate of 103 %. At higher concentrations of 6.0 µM and 8.0 µM, the measured concentrations were 6.2 µM and 8.1 µM, leading to recovery rates of 103 % and 101 %, respectively. These results highlight the reliable accuracy of the OFL-S-CD composite, even in complex sample matrices like tap water, and demonstrate its resilience to ion interference.

The synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (S-CDs) from sumac exhibited a significant enhancement in fluorescence and a notable red-shift in emission upon interaction with OFL, emphasizing their strong optical response. This dual effect confirms the effective binding mechanism and highlights the potential of S-CDs as highly sensitive probes for OFL detection. A dual-mode detection system was developed, combining conventional fluorometry with a smartphone-assisted visual detection method, offering a versatile and practical approach for OFL sensing in real-world applications. The instrument-based sensor demonstrated very sensitive detection with LOD as small as 0.7 nM and linear detection range of 1–90 nM was calculated. Furthermore, the smartphone-based RGB analysis enabled real-time, cost-effective quantification of OFL, exhibiting a linear detection range of 20–80 µM with a LOD of 0.97 µM. These findings validate the potential of S-CDs as a portable and efficient sensing platform for pharmaceutical quality control and environmental monitoring. Moreover, the successful application of the OFL-S-CD composite in liquid formulations, including eye drops, highlights its potential for drug delivery and diagnostic applications. Further research is warranted to explore its broader applications in pharmaceutical formulations and environmental monitoring, reinforcing the utility of sumac-derived carbon dots in biomedical and analytical fields.
Shilan A. Fatah: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Khalid M. Omer: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
